findings.description: asyncio is imported but never used (the code calls blocking time.sleep instead of asyncio.sleep), leaving a dead import.
JobQueue is not thread-safe; if push/pop are ever called from multiple threads the check-then-pop in pop() can race on the underlying list.
list.pop(0) is O(n) per dequeue making a full drain O(n^2); collections.deque.popleft() would be O(1).
json.dumps(job.payload) raises TypeError for non-JSON-serializable payload values (payload is dict[str, Any]), turning a logging call into a job failure that then gets retried.
The full job payload is logged verbatim, which can leak sensitive data (credentials, tokens, PII) into log files.
Missing source/dest payload keys silently default to empty strings, producing a malformed 'convert' command that fails downstream instead of a clear validation error.
The 'convert' binary is resolved via PATH rather than an absolute path, so a manipulated PATH (or Windows' built-in convert.exe) executes the wrong binary.

Untrusted payload fields source/dest are interpolated into a shell command executed with shell=True, allowing arbitrary command injection (e.g. source='; rm -rf ~'); use an argument list with shell=False or shlex.quote.
subprocess.run has no timeout, so a hung convert process blocks the worker (and, via poll_loop, the whole event loop) indefinitely.
capture_output=True is set but stdout/stderr are never examined or logged on failure, discarding the only diagnostics for why a convert job failed.
An unknown job kind is treated as a retryable failure and goes through the full retry/backoff path instead of being rejected permanently (no dead-letter/poison handling).

'attempt = 0' inside the loop resets the counter every iteration, so the while condition never reaches MAX_RETRIES: any permanently failing job retries forever (infinite loop with constant 2s backoff) and run_with_retries never returns False.
The backoff sleep executes even on what should be the final attempt (sleep happens before the loop-exit check), pointlessly delaying the permanent-failure result once the counter-reset bug is fixed.
Blocking time.sleep inside the async poll_loop coroutine freezes the entire event loop every idle poll; it must be 'await asyncio.sleep(POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS)'.
When stop_after is set but the queue is empty, the 'continue' skips the stop check so poll_loop sleeps forever and never returns, hanging callers/tests that expect termination.

run_with_retries performs blocking sleeps and subprocess calls directly on the event loop thread (no await/executor offload), starving all other coroutines for the duration of each job and its retries.

stop_after is compared against the cumulative instance counters processed+failed, which are never reset, so a worker that already handled jobs (e.g. via drain_sync) stops early or returns immediately instead of counting jobs handled in this invocation.
Return type is annotated as bare 'dict' instead of 'dict[str, int]', losing type information for callers.
drain_sync returns the lifetime cumulative counters rather than counts for this drain call, which is misleading when the CLI invokes it more than once on the same worker.
